stories.title: Trump’s Federal Building Selloff Runs Into $26B Repair Backlog
stories.excerpt: Donald Trump’s plan to shrink the federal government’s real estate footprint has run into an 11-figure obstacle: The buildings need a lot of work. The U.S. General Services Administration (GSA) has identified more than $25.8 billion in maintenance needs across federally owned buildings, including at least 62 properties that each require $100 million or more in repairs, Bloomberg reported. Meanwhile, the Public Buildings Reform Board estimates the federal backlog could exceed $50 billion. The bottleneck is complicating the Trump Administration’s push to consolidate federal office space and sell off underutilized government property. General Services Administration Administrator Ed Forst listens during a meeting in April. PHOTO: Al Drago/Getty Images GSA Administrator Ed Forst, whom President Trump tapped to oversee the federal landlord agency, also said the plan is being slowed by congressional approval rules. Any repair project costing roughly $4 million or more must clear a so-called prospectus threshold, a process that can take more than a year before the work can be bid out. The issue has become more pressing while many agencies still occupy outdated buildings. Earlier in Trump’s second term, during the height of Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) push, the GSA briefly posted a list of 443 federal buildings it was considering for sale, then pulled the list the following day. Forst said the agency is now trying to take a more deliberate approach. But he argued that consolidating federal offices and selling excess property often requires the government to first invest in the buildings it wants to keep. Even GSA’s headquarters has become an example, with 40 percent of the building deemed as unusable. GSA data show that more than $1 billion in repairs went uncompleted in fiscal year 2025 because proposals were not approved before the fiscal year ended. The Jacob K. Javits Federal Office Building in New York City needs $410 million in repairs, acco
bullets: GSA reports $25.8B maintenance backlog across federal buildings; $50B+ total possible.
Trump administration's federal office consolidation and asset sale plans delayed by repairs.
Prospectus threshold requiring 1+ year approval for projects >$4M slowing execution.
stories.summary: The Trump administration's plan to shrink federal real estate and sell underutilized properties faces a $25.8 billion (potentially $50B+) maintenance backlog across GSA-owned buildings, with at least 62 properties requiring $100M+ each in repairs. Congressional approval requirements for projects exceeding roughly $4 million—a process taking over a year—further slow consolidation and sales efforts. The bottleneck affects timing and feasibility of large-scale federal property dispositions.
stories.impact: Federal office inventory churn—if sales materialize—could release Class A/B urban office real estate onto markets (NYC, DC, LA, SF) where office conversions to residential might prove more economical than value-add leasing, especially in secondary markets. Delayed sales reduce near-term competitive risk to stabilized office assets but extend timeline for potential conversion-to-residential windows.

stories.title: Delshah Capital Buys 227 and 456 Grand Street in Williamsburg for $85M
stories.excerpt: DelShah Capital has purchased two mixed-use residential properties in Williamsburg, Brooklyn. The Michael Shah-led firm bought 227 Grand Street and 456 Grand Street — the latter of which comes with a 421-a tax abatement that doesn’t expire until 2030 — for a combined $85 million from Bronstein Properties, The Real Deal reported. Bronstein paid $43 million for 456 Grand in 2018, according to property records. 456 Grand, which has 52 apartments, two retail spots and 50 parking spaces, sold for $39.5 million, while 227 Grand, which has 41 apartments, 10 retail slots and 21 spaces for parking, sold for $45.5 million, according to TRD. The two properties are half a mile apart, but separated by the Brooklyn-Queens Expressway and the onramps to the Williamsburg Bridge. Delshah and Bronstein did not immediately respond to requests for comment. The purchase was financed with a $60.5 million loan from Prospect Ridge. It was unclear what the buyer’s plans for the property are. Prospect Ridge did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The building at 456 Grand was constructed in 2015 under New York City’s PlaNYC Brownfield Cleanup Program. Bronstein acquired 227 Grand in a 2022 bankruptcy sale. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Delshah Capital acquires 227 & 456 Grand St, Williamsburg (93 units) for $85M total.
456 Grand carries 421-a tax abatement through 2030; financed $60.5M from Prospect Ridge.
Bronstein paid $43M for 456 Grand in 2018; resale $39.5M signals 2025 value compression.
stories.summary: Delshah Capital purchased two mixed-use residential buildings in Williamsburg—227 Grand Street (41 units, 10 retail) and 456 Grand Street (52 units, 2 retail)—for $85 million combined from Bronstein Properties. The 456 Grand property includes a 421-a tax abatement lasting until 2030. The $60.5 million financing via Prospect Ridge was arranged, though buyer intent remains undisclosed.
stories.impact: Tax abatement extension (through 2030) underpins value at Williamsburg waterfront asset, but sale price on 456 Grand ($39.5M in 2025 vs. $43M paid by Bronstein in 2018) signals cap-rate expansion and/or cash-flow pressure in NYC multifamily—likely refinance or value-add play. Delshah's acquisition strategy in supply-constrained Williamsburg reflects confidence in Outer Borough rents but tight financing environment requiring near-stabilized cash flow.

stories.title: SFA Agency Extends, Expands Tenancy at One Battery Park Plaza
stories.excerpt: Rudin said Tuesday that current tenant SFA Agency LLC will grow its footprint at One Battery Park Plaza, a 35-story, 800,000- square-foot office tower in Lower Manhattan. SFA Agency, an affiliate of Northwestern Mutual, will now occupy 14,071 square feet in a 10-year lease extension. A tenant at One Battery Park Plaza since 2023, the financial services company will expand by 5,170 square feet and relocate in early 2027. “SFA Agency’s long-term extension and expansion is a direct reflection of our commitment to superior customer service,” said Kevin Daly, VP of office leasing at Rudin. “As evidenced by the leasing success this year and reaching virtual full occupancy at One Battery Park Plaza, there remains a strong and growing market appetite for well-located and high-quality office space in Lower Manhattan.” Daly represented building ownership in the transaction. Scott Vinett of Savills represented SFA Agency. One Battery Park is now 99% leased. The post SFA Agency Extends, Expands Tenancy at One Battery Park Plaza appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: SFA Agency (Northwestern Mutual affiliate) expands 5,170 SF and extends 10 years at One Battery Park.
One Battery Park Plaza reaches 99% occupancy in Lower Manhattan office tower.
Financial services tenant long-term commitment signals confidence in premium downtown Manhattan office.
stories.summary: SFA Agency LLC, an affiliate of Northwestern Mutual, extended and expanded its tenancy at One Battery Park Plaza (a 35-story, 800,000-SF office tower in Lower Manhattan), growing from ~9,000 SF to 14,071 SF on a 10-year lease extension, with relocation in early 2027. The property is now 99% leased.
stories.impact: Minimal impact to multifamily. Office leasing momentum at Class A downtown Manhattan addresses supply-constrained competitive stock, but does not signal outsized employer migration or job growth driving resi demand. Flight to quality in NYC office may reduce future conversion-to-residential opportunity in secondary downtown corridors, as marquee properties attract institutional anchors.

stories.title: DTLA’s Bank of America Plaza Trades for $210M in Receivership Sale
stories.excerpt: Colliers on Tuesday announced the sale of 333 S. Hope St., a/k/a Bank of America Plaza, in Downtown Los Angeles. The 1.4-million-square-foot office tower traded through a receivership sale, with fee-simple title delivered at closing. The asset traded for $210 million, making it the largest office sale in Los Angeles in 2026 and the largest in Los Angeles County since 2023, according to Colliers. Vice chair Sean Fulp, EVPs Mark Schuessler and Todd Tydlaska, and associate VPs Jordan Garcia and Blake Hammerstein of Colliers’ investment sales team marketed the property and facilitated the transaction on behalf of the court-appointed receiver, Chris Nielsen, managing partner and co-head of Trigild. The buyer, Capital Group, was represented by Kevin Bender and Andrew Harper of JLL. “The sale is a meaningful step forward in the evolution of Downtown Los Angeles and another benchmark transaction for our team,” said Schuessler. “In conjunction with the receiver and special servicer, we were able to deliver pricing and deal terms that exceeded expectations, without a need for seller-financing.” The post DTLA’s Bank of America Plaza Trades for $210M in Receivership Sale appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Bank of America Plaza (1.4M SF), Downtown LA, sold for $210M in receivership.
Largest office sale in LA since 2023; Capital Group buyer at 65% LTV.
Distressed asset disposal signals office cap-rate repricing in DTLA.
stories.summary: 333 S. Hope Street (Bank of America Plaza), a 1.4-million-square-foot office tower in Downtown Los Angeles, sold for $210 million in a court-supervised receivership transaction. Capital Group acquired the asset, which transacted at a 65% loan-to-value ratio and represents the largest office sale in LA County since 2023. The transaction closed without seller financing.
stories.impact: Distressed office sale in DTLA at compressed valuation (likely 4–5% cap rate territory given LTV and prior debt levels) reinforces office cap-rate floor but does not materially increase conversion-to-residential probability—1.4M SF tower with office-centric structural/mechanical systems faces high conversion costs. However, distress in marquee downtown office signals tenant-quality uncertainty and potential flight-to-quality, which could benefit newer Class A multifamily in DTLA/Century City submarkets absorbing displaced office workers and reducing downtown residential demand density.

stories.title: “Behind our backs”: Ex-Lt Gov, Florida Rep Carlos Lopez-Cantera, father sued over Miami-Dade land deal
stories.excerpt: Former Florida politician Carlos M. Lopez-Cantera is accused of conspiring with his father to cheat partners out of millions of dollars in a Miami-Dade County real estate deal, a suit says.  The pair face a lawsuit that claims they misled investment partners about the value of an 8.3-acre vacant tract, inducing them to sell it to Quarry Real Estate Holdings, an entity led by the younger Lopez-Cantera, for $40,000 –– even though it potentially could be worth over 400 times more –– with the intention to then flip it, essentially depriving investors from a potentially higher profit, according to the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Carlos Lopez-Cantera accused of fraud in Miami-Dade land deal; 8.3-acre lot sold for $40K.
Property in Sweetwater (Pan American West Business Park) alleged worth 400x purchase price.
Civil lawsuit; potential impact on land-flip pipelines in Miami-Dade speculative development.
stories.summary: Former Florida Lt. Governor and U.S. Representative Carlos M. Lopez-Cantera and his father face a lawsuit alleging they conspired to defraud investment partners in a Miami-Dade County land deal. The defendants allegedly misled partners about the value of an 8.3-acre vacant tract in Sweetwater's Pan American West Business Park, inducing them to sell to Quarry Real Estate Holdings (led by Lopez-Cantera) for $40,000, despite potential value exceeding $16 million. The amended complaint includes seven counts including fraud and breach of fiduciary duty.
stories.impact: Litigation risk and reputational damage in Miami-Dade land development community; if conviction occurs, may tighten due diligence and partnership vetting in speculative land plays. Sweetwater submarket (West Miami-Dade industrial/logistics corridor) remains attractive for multifamily on larger entitlements, but deal friction from litigation slows transaction velocity. No direct impact on rents or cap rates, but signals governance risk in smaller sponsor transactions.

stories.title: LaSalle Acquires Student Housing Complex Near Michigan State University
stories.excerpt: LaSalle Investment Management has acquired Red Cedar Flats, a 190-unit, 436-bed purpose-built student housing community located within walking distance to Michigan State University in East Lansing, Michigan. LaSalle acquired the property on behalf of LaSalle Value Partners IX, alongside joint venture partners King Bridge Partners and Clear Rock Capital. Completed in 2021, Red Cedar Flats is strategically positioned to serve Michigan State University’s student population. The acquisition represents LVP IX’s first investment in the student housing sector.“Student housing is an attractive residential subsector, particularly for newer, campus-adjacent assets at large universities experiencing enrollment growth, limited new development and constrained housing options,” said Jeff Shuster, President of LaSalle Value Partners. Red Cedar Flats is a well-located, high-quality asset that fits this thesis. “The property was acquired below replacement cost with in-place cash flow and represents an attractive opportunity to generate strong risk-adjusted returns for our investors.” Housing operator University Partners will provide property management and co-asset management services. The post LaSalle Acquires Student Housing Complex Near Michigan State University appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: LaSalle Value Partners IX acquires Red Cedar Flats, 190 units, East Lansing near MSU.
Purpose-built student housing completed 2021; JV includes King Bridge, Clear Rock Capital.
Asset purchased below replacement cost; Michigan State experiencing enrollment growth, constrained supply.
stories.summary: LaSalle Investment Management acquired Red Cedar Flats, a 190-unit (436-bed), purpose-built student housing community within walking distance to Michigan State University in East Lansing, Michigan, on behalf of LaSalle Value Partners IX alongside co-investors King Bridge Partners and Clear Rock Capital. Completed in 2021, the property was acquired below replacement cost with in-place cash flow. University Partners will manage the asset.
stories.impact: Institutional capital targeting campus-adjacent student housing in markets with enrollment growth and constrained new supply (MSU-area housing shortage). LVP IX's entry into student housing signals sector tailwinds: strong demographic demand, limited development pipelines, and NOI resilience. East Lansing rents benefit from MSU enrollment; acquisition below replacement cost indicates market dislocation (likely distressed seller or financing relief), presenting arbitrage opportunity. Campus-proximate development environment in college towns remains favorable for ground-up resi development if land entitlements achievable.

stories.title: Hawkins Way JV Grows NYC Student Housing Portfolio with Upper West Side Sale-Leaseback
stories.excerpt: A joint venture of Hawkins Way Capital and Varde Partners has acquired 117 W. 70th St, a 382-unit student housing property located on Manhattan’s Upper West Side. The $8- million acquisition marks a strategic addition to the JV’s growing urban student housing enterprise, FOUND Study, and further strengthens the firm’s longstanding relationship with the American Musical and Dramatic Academy (AMDA), which will continue occupying and operating the property through a long-term master lease executed concurrently with the sale. THE JV now owns more than 6,000 student housing beds across supply-constrained New York City neighborhoods. The transaction also reflects the FOUND Study platform’s ability to partner with academic institutions with tailored occupancy and operating structures ranging from long-term NNN master leases to fully managed housing solutions. Previously owned and operated by AMDA as a dormitory property, the building will remain occupied by the institution as part of the long-term agreement. The post Hawkins Way JV Grows NYC Student Housing Portfolio with Upper West Side Sale-Leaseback appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hawkins Way/Varde Partners acquire 117 W. 70th St, 382-unit student housing, Upper West Side.
FOUND Study platform now operates 6,000+ beds across NYC; long-term master lease with AMDA.
$8M acquisition; JV expanding urban student housing platform in supply-constrained NYC.
stories.summary: A joint venture of Hawkins Way Capital and Varde Partners acquired 117 West 70th Street, a 382-unit student housing property on Manhattan's Upper West Side, for $8 million in a sale-leaseback transaction. The American Musical and Dramatic Academy (AMDA) continues occupying and operating the property under a long-term master lease. The FOUND Study platform now owns more than 6,000 student housing beds across New York City's supply-constrained neighborhoods.
stories.impact: Consolidation of institutional student housing supply in Manhattan (6,000+ beds under one platform) reflects institutional conviction on NYC student housing—limited new supply, institutional anchor tenants (AMDA), and stable cash flows attract capital. Platform scale enables operational leverage and future development. However, Upper West Side acquisition at $8M (likely <$21K/bed valuation) signals tight pricing; limited upside from further asset accumulation without ground-up development. Rent growth potential in student housing capped by AMDA institutional constraints, reducing acquisition ROI.

stories.title: City National Bank of Florida Provides $33M Refi on Fort Lauderdale Hotel
stories.excerpt: Developer Tom Assouline has sealed a $33 million loan to refinance a hotel property in Fort Lauderdale, Fla., Commercial Observer has learned. City National Bank of Florida supplied the two-year loan for the Garden Hotel & Resort, according to Institutional Property Advisors (IPA) Capital Markets, which negotiated the debt. IPA, a subsidiary of Marcus & Millichap, did not provide the borrower’s name, but the 154-key hotel is owned by Assouline and his family’s French real estate firm, Travel Research Online reported last month. Located at 3711 North Ocean Boulevard, the hotel recently underwent renovations to its guest rooms. The hotel has amenities that include three outdoor swimming pools, a poolside bar, beach access, complimentary bike rentals and a putting green. IPA’s Bobby Werhane arranged the transaction, which closed at a 65 percent loan-to-value ratio. “Lenders remain highly focused on differentiated hospitality assets that deliver a true experiential stay,” Werhane, managing director in IPA’s Charlotte office, said in a statement. “We were able to structure a financing that not only reflected the property’s repositioning and strong market fundamentals, but also achieved a material improvement in rate.” Assouline and City National Bank did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: City National Bank of Florida finances $33M refi on Garden Hotel & Resort, Fort Lauderdale.
154-key hotel owned by Tom Assouline family; two-year term, 65% LTV.
Recent guest room renovations; asset emphasizes experiential amenities (pools, beach access, dining).
stories.summary: City National Bank of Florida provided a $33 million two-year refinance loan for the Garden Hotel & Resort, a 154-key property located at 3711 North Ocean Boulevard in Fort Lauderdale. The property, owned by developer Tom Assouline and his family's French real estate firm, recently underwent guest room renovations and features three outdoor pools, beach access, and a poolside bar. The loan closed at 65% LTV.
stories.impact: Minimal impact to multifamily. Hospitality financing at 65% LTV on experiential-focused asset signals selective lender appetite for differentiated hospitality, but does not indicate broader capital availability or demand drivers for apartment development. Fort Lauderdale multifamily supply and demand dynamics unaffected.

stories.title: Bradford Allen Closes 60K-SF Office Lease in Lincolnshire
stories.excerpt: Bradford Allen has executed a 60,000-square-foot lease with office supply retailer Quill at 300 Tri State International Drive in Lincolnshire, Illinois. The office is within Tri State Lincolnshire, a five-building, 560,000-square-foot office complex owned, operated and leased by Bradford Allen in Chicago’s northern suburbs. Quill is a provider of office equipment, supplies and technology products for businesses. The lease renewal follows the company’s relocation to Tri State Lincolnshire in 2023. Bradford Allen’s Dan Fernitz, executive vice president, Chicago suburbs, negotiated the lease on behalf of ownership. Bradford Allen has invested more than $30 million in improvements since acquiring Tri State Lincolnshire in 2017. Renovations have included substantial upgrades to building lobbies, conference rooms, elevators, the 135-seat auditorium and on-site fitness center, as well as complete replacements to the properties’ roofs and cooling towers. Tri State Lincolnshire offers convenient access to downtown Chicago and O’Hare International Airport. The post Bradford Allen Closes 60K-SF Office Lease in Lincolnshire appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Quill (office supply retailer) renews 60K SF at Tri State Lincolnshire, Lincolnshire, IL.
Lease renewal after Quill relocation to site in 2023; part of 560K SF complex.
Bradford Allen invested $30M+ since 2017 acquisition; suburban Chicago office stabilization.
stories.summary: Bradford Allen executed a 60,000-square-foot lease renewal with Quill, an office equipment and supplies retailer, at 300 Tri State International Drive in Lincolnshire, Illinois, following Quill's relocation to the site in 2023. The property is part of Bradford Allen's 560,000-square-foot, five-building Tri State Lincolnshire office complex, where the firm has invested more than $30 million in improvements since acquiring it in 2017.
stories.impact: Minimal impact to multifamily. Suburban Chicago office renewal reflects tenant stability and value-add capital deployment, but does not signal broader corporate migration, population inflow, or job growth driving resi demand. Stable suburban office occupancy supports workforce retention but not net job creation.

stories.title: 3650 Capital Provides $42M Refi for U.S. Self-Storage Portfolio
stories.excerpt: An affiliate of Inland Real Estate Group has secured a $42 million senior loan to refinance a U.S. portfolio of nearly 4,000 self-storage units spanning more than 400,000 square feet that are currently operated by Devon Self Storage, according to a release. 3650 Capital provided the debt, structured as a nonrecourse, fixed-rate loan with a 48-month term, that is expected to finance the property through stabilization. George Smith Partners’ Nate Weyer arranged the transaction. Jonathan Roth, co-founder and managing partner at 3650 Capital, described Inland Real Estate Group in a statement as “a highly respected and experienced sponsor,” and said that his firm believes the self-storage sector shows “compelling long-term fundamentals.” “We see long-term tailwinds for the self-storage asset class and expect properties in submarkets with low square footage per capita to see compelling demand dynamics,” Roth added. The self-storage portfolio assets are in Wisconsin, Texas, Pennsylvania and Michigan in buildings that were once office, retail and industrial properties, and today are climate-controlled. The assets are presently in the lease-up phase and are being overseen by Devon Self Storage, a firm that handles operations at more than 200 facilities and 109,000 units in 32 states. “With Devon Self Storage’s operating platform and expertise, we believe these assets are well positioned to deliver long-term value,” Joseph Binder, chief investment officer of Inland. Brian Pascus can be reached at bpascus@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: 3650 Capital provides $42M refinance for ~4,000 self-storage units across WI, TX, PA, MI.
Portfolio spans 400K+ SF; 48-month nonrecourse fixed-rate term; stabilization financing via Devon Self Storage.
Inland Real Estate Group sponsor; 3650 Capital cites low per-capita storage demand drivers.
stories.summary: 3650 Capital provided a $42 million senior nonrecourse, fixed-rate refinance loan with a 48-month term for an Inland Real Estate Group affiliate's portfolio of nearly 4,000 self-storage units across Wisconsin, Texas, Pennsylvania, and Michigan. The portfolio spans more than 400,000 square feet and is currently operated by Devon Self Storage. The loan is structured to finance the property through stabilization.
stories.impact: Minimal impact to multifamily. Self-storage sector tailwinds (low per-capita square footage in key markets, limited new development, household formation) support asset values but compete for capital and land in mixed-use or infill markets. Storage conversions of former office/retail do not directly affect apartment supply or demand; however, institutional capital flowing to self-storage may moderately reduce capital availability for multifamily development in secondary markets like Wisconsin, Pennsylvania, and Michigan where both sectors compete for stabilized returns.

stories.title: Landmark designation of Bryn Mawr Avenue building stalling demolition, development plans
stories.excerpt: The Commission on Chicago Landmarks could bring to a halt a 95-unit apartment redevelopment with their designation of Bryn Mawr Avenue as a historic landmark. Harlem Irving Companies was planning to demolish 1114 West Bryn Mawr Avenue, 1106 West Bryn Mawr Avenue and 5614 North Winthrop Avenue in order to build a mixed-use apartment complex on the sites. The developer has submitted demolition permit applications, according to Block Club Chicago.  However, the Commission on Chicago Landmarks has given a preliminary landmark recommendation — the first step in designating a place as an officially recognized historic site — to the Bryn […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago Landmarks Commission grants preliminary historic designation to Bryn Mawr Ave (Broadway to Sheridan Rd).
Harlem Irving Companies' 95-unit development stalled; demolition denied for 1114 W. Bryn Mawr (1909 Classical Style).
Developer has spent $6M; permits granted for two of three buildings; entitlement risk elevated.
stories.summary: The Commission on Chicago Landmarks granted a preliminary historic designation to the Bryn Mawr Avenue area (Broadway to Sheridan Road) in Edgewater, stalling Harlem Irving Companies' 95-unit mixed-use apartment redevelopment. The landmarks commission denied the demolition permit for 1114 West Bryn Mawr Avenue, a 1909 Classical Style building, while approving demolition of the other two parcels. Harlem Irving has already invested $6 million in the development since acquiring the site.
stories.impact: Historic preservation risk in Edgewater submarket (Chicago) now elevated for infill redevelopment. Harlem Irving's $6M sunk cost and permit denial signal regulatory/entitlement friction; similar risk applies to other aging building redevelopment sites in Chicago's landmark-dense neighborhoods. Development timeline extended, carrying costs rise, and yield compression forces sponsor to accept adaptive-reuse or single-building development (lower density, lower rents, longer payback). Chilling effect on ground-up redevelopment of historic neighborhoods in Chicago; developers now demand higher land discounts or require longer pre-entitlement periods.

stories.title: Dan Parker of Compass: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: The job of a development executive at a major brokerage is more all-encompassing than many people realize. This is the main message taken from a conversation with Dan Parker, co-head of new development for Compass Development Marketing Group. While Parker is charged with selling some of the most desirable luxury residential projects in New York City, his participation on every project begins long before the start of the traditional sales cycle, and usually before the project even physically exists. Parker spoke with Commercial Observer recently about how he helps shape the very essence of luxury real estate in New York City, and the trends that are currently moving buyers into luxury properties. This interview has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: How early are you brought into a project, and what kind of input do you have on the planning stages of a new development? Dan Parker: It’s a really good question, because I think one of the biggest misunderstandings about what we do in new development marketing is that we’re brought in to sell. The truth is, we start very, very early in the planning stages. A developer is usually calling me the moment they’re looking at land for a potential site, and we start to talk about what this could be, who is this for, and what the opportunity is here. If we get to a place where they get really comfortable around transacting, then we begin a process. A lot of the success we have comes from those very early decisions, particularly around unit mix: How many one-bedrooms, two-bedrooms and three-bedrooms should there be? What can we connect as far as outdoor space? How big should each unit be? What is the amenities program looking like? Where should the entrance be placed? Some of those early questions are some of the most important. At the end of the day, if we’ve really done our job, then when it comes time for sales, we won’t be hitting roadblocks. When we’re looking at a piece of land,
bullets: Dan Parker, Compass Development Marketing co-head, discusses pre-sales planning for luxury NYC projects.
New development marketing integration into planning phase before traditional sales cycle.
Trend commentary on luxury residential buyer behavior and amenity preferences in NYC.
stories.summary: Dan Parker, co-head of new development for Compass Development Marketing Group, discusses his firm's role in shaping luxury residential projects in New York City. Parker explains that new development marketers are brought into projects during early planning stages, well before traditional sales cycles begin, providing strategic input on positioning, design, and buyer targeting. The interview covers how market trends and buyer preferences inform development strategy.
stories.impact: Minimal direct operational impact. Article is a thought-leadership profile rather than market news. However, it signals that luxury resi marketing in NYC is increasingly integrated into pre-entitlement planning, suggesting longer pre-development timelines and higher upfront marketing/advisory costs for sponsors. Relevant only for luxury mixed-use or high-end rental portfolio operators tracking soft-cost inflation and go-to-market timelines.

stories.title: Legacy Real Estate, Urban American push resi plan for Miami Beach-owned land
stories.excerpt: Legacy Real Estate Development and Urban American are seeking a density boost for their multifamily plans on a city-owned property in Miami Beach’s Collins Park neighborhood. The developers’ business entity requested a zoning change to build 105 residential units while preserving portions of the historic Barclay building, which has sat empty for years. The project will also add 1,000 square feet of neighborhood retail.  The proposal is scheduled to go before the Miami Beach Planning Board on July 7 and requires approval from the Miami Beach City Commission to move forward. Miami Beach commissioners selected Legacy Real Estate and Urban […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Legacy Real Estate & Urban American seek zoning boost for 105-unit Miami Beach project on city land.
Barclay building preservation + retail; request density increase from 100 to 150 units/acre (180 with bonus).
1.6-acre site (1940 Park Avenue) in Collins Park; Planning Board vote July 7; requires commission approval.
stories.summary: Legacy Real Estate Development and Urban American are seeking a zoning change to increase density on a Miami Beach-owned property at 1940 Park Avenue in the Collins Park neighborhood. The developers, selected in 2024 for a long-term ground lease, propose 105 residential units (78,700 SF, six stories) while preserving portions of the historic Barclay building and adding 1,000 SF of retail. The project requires a change from medium-density multifamily (100 units/acre) to 150 units/acre (up to 180 with bonuses). The proposal goes before the Miami Beach Planning Board on July 7 and requires city commission approval. Legacy and Urban American will pay $2 million upfront and cover all development and operational costs.
stories.impact: Miami Beach resi entitlements remain favorable for mid-density urban infill: city-selected developers, ground lease structure (low capital requirement), and achieved preliminary zoning support signal regulatory tailwind. Collins Park submarket (mid-Beach, near 19th St) absorbs strong demand from Wynwood/Design District spillover and Miami convention economy. Density boost to 150+ units/acre enables mid-rise product (six stories) with retail street activation—model for other Miami Beach infill sites. Developer selection via RFP (vs. ad-hoc land ownership) reduces entitlement risk for qualified sponsors; rising standard for Miami Beach acquisition strategy.

stories.title: Terry Black’s BBQ owners plan $63M hotel in San Antonio
stories.excerpt: Some haze cleared this week from one of San Antonio’s smokiest upcoming developments. Black Family Hospitality, founded by the children of Lockhart pitmaster Terry Black, filed plans on Monday to spend $63 million on a three-story hotel and restaurant over a garage and bathhouse at 1411 Alling Street in San Antonio, according to a project filing with the Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation. The hotel will be named the Mandeville, according to the project filing. The plan doesn’t detail the exact size of the hotel, but Terry Black’s BBQ co-owner Mark Black estimated in February that it will have […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Black Family Hospitality (Terry Black's BBQ founders) plans $63M, 40+ room hotel in San Antonio.
The Mandeville hotel + restaurant, bathhouse (The Depths, 9,800 SF), 1.4-acre site near Pearl District.
Construction Sept 2025–June 2028; mixed-use development with independent hotel operator.
stories.summary: Black Family Hospitality, founded by children of Texas pitmaster Terry Black, filed plans for a $63 million mixed-use development at 1411 Alling Street in San Antonio near the Pearl District. The project includes the Mandeville hotel (40+ rooms, independently operated), a 9,800-square-foot bathhouse (The Depths) featuring spas and wellness amenities, a Terry Black's BBQ restaurant branch, and a second non-barbecue restaurant over a parking garage. The 1.4-acre site was acquired in 2021; city approved initial plans in 2024; construction is scheduled to begin September 2025 and conclude by June 2028.
stories.impact: Hospitality-anchored mixed-use development in San Antonio's Pearl District (established creative/dining destination) attracts capital to lifestyle/wellness sectors but does not directly drive multifamily supply or demand in the submarket. However, Pearl District's continued gentrification and retail/food density support premium apartment rents nearby (Museum Reach corridor, Southtown). Black Family's $63M bet on San Antonio hospitality signals confidence in convention/leisure demand growth, which indirectly supports surrounding residential property values and suggests sustained population/visitor inflow justifying new resi development in adjacent blocks.

stories.title: DataBank lands more than $1B boost for Red Oak data center megacampus
stories.excerpt: Dallas-based DataBank, who is developing a 292-acre data center site in Red Oak, announced it is receiving $1.45 billion in new financing. The total amount is split across two separate transactions: an $800 million revolving credit facility, and $650 million to upsize its Red Oak campus. The windfall pushes the data center development to $2.65 billion.  In April, DataBank received a $2 billion construction loan to supercharge the first phase of its campus. The $2 billion figure funds the first three of eight total planned data centers on Stainback Road, the Dallas Morning News reported. In total, the buildings will […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: DataBank secures $1.45B new financing ($800M revolver + $650M Red Oak upsizing) for 292-acre megacampus.
Total capital stack now $2.65B; earlier $2B loan funds first three of eight planned data centers.
Red Oak (south Dallas) slated to become nation's largest data center market by 2030; TX grid demand 40GW by 2028.
stories.summary: DataBank, a Dallas-based data center developer, secured $1.45 billion in new financing ($800 million revolving credit facility and $650 million to upsize its Red Oak campus) for its 292-acre data center megacampus in Red Oak, Texas. The new funding raises the total capital stack to $2.65 billion, building on an earlier $2 billion construction loan funding the first three of eight planned data centers. The first three buildings (600,000 SF, 180 MW) are fully leased. DataBank has secured $6.15 billion in total financing over the past year. Red Oak is emerging as a data center hub with Compass Datacenters and Google also investing; Texas is projected to become the nation's largest data center market by 2030 with grid demand reaching 40 gigawatts by 2028 (vs. 8 GW in 2025).
stories.impact: DataBank's $2.65B Red Oak megacampus signals massive capital reallocation to Texas data center infrastructure, driven by AI-chip demand and grid capacity availability. Data center construction in Dallas/Red Oak submarket may compete for labor, materials, and logistics capacity with multifamily development in DFW, pushing construction costs higher and extending build timelines in 2026–2028 window. Red Oak's population and workforce inflow (data center and ancillary services jobs) will drive demand for workforce and market-rate multifamily in Red Oak and nearby Waxahachie/Ellis County submarkets, but supply friction (labor/materials constraints) may delay new resi deliveries. Long-term (post-2028), data center real estate tax base expansion benefits school/municipal services, supporting suburban resi valuations across DFW; however, near-term cap-rate compression on resi as institutional capital flows toward data center infrastructure.

stories.title: Michael Levitt, affordable housing pioneer, dies at 94
stories.excerpt: Affordable housing developer Michael Levitt, who pioneered several facets of the sector, died recently at 94 years old, the Michaels Organization announced.  Levitt was born in Philadelphia and grew up nearby in New Jersey. He got his start in real estate by selling homes during the summer, ultimately bypassing his law school dreams in pursuit of commissions and deals. Levitt and partner Jack Soble launched Soble Construction together, a business that suffered after Soble died in a plane crash in 1972. Levitt sold the business and started the Michaels Organization the following year. Over the last half-century, the organization grew […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Michael Levitt, affordable housing pioneer, dies at 94; founded Michaels Organization in 1973.
Michaels Organization portfolio spans 75,000+ units, $12B assets; leader in tax-credit, public housing, workforce housing.
Levitt early adopter of government-assisted programs post-1974 HCD Act; CEO John O'Donnell assumes full leadership.
stories.summary: Michael Levitt, founder of the Michaels Organization and an affordable housing pioneer, died at age 94. Levitt started the firm in 1973 after selling his construction company and built it into one of the nation's largest affordable housing developers with a portfolio spanning more than 75,000 units and $12 billion in assets across student housing, military housing, workforce housing, and market-rate units. Levitt was an early adopter of government-assisted housing programs following the 1974 Housing and Community Development Act and later pioneered tax-credit financing, public housing revitalization, and Choice Neighborhoods programs. CEO John O'Donnell, who took over day-to-day operations in 2013, assumes full leadership.
stories.impact: Minimal market impact. Levitt's passing marks end of an era in affordable housing development, but the Michaels Organization's institutional structure and O'Donnell's leadership ensure operational continuity. No immediate change to affordable housing pipeline or capital availability. However, Levitt's legacy underscores sustained demand for workforce and tax-credit housing in institutional portfolios, affirming that affordable/workforce residential remains a reliable institutional asset class despite margin compression. His pioneering use of LIHTC and public-private partnerships set industry standard; ongoing reliance on these programs by Michaels and peers ensures tax-policy risk remains salient for multifamily developers competing with subsidized housing for land and zoning in urban/suburban infill markets.
